refined_fr.refined_fr: Enable measurement and reporting of material throughput in volumetric or mass units per unit time.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the system's capability to measure/report throughput metrics, and (2) the achievement of a customer-defined target. The latter is a success criterion dependent on operational execution, not a system requirement. The refined FR isolates the system's functional obligation—providing visibility into throughput—which is solution-neutral and testable independent of whether a specific productivity target is met. The target itself belongs in acceptance criteria or KPI definitions, not in the FR.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that the NGHE solution captures and reports material throughput (m³/h or ton/h) during representative operations. Verify data accuracy against independent measurement. Confirm reporting is accessible to Site Manager and relevant stakeholders in real time or near-real time.
sub_frs: Acquire material throughput data (volume or mass per time interval) during equipment operation.
Calculate and aggregate throughput metrics from acquired data.
Report throughput metrics to authorized stakeholders in a human-readable format.

candidate_dps: DP1: Continuous volumetric flow measurement via inline sensor with real-time signal conditioning and local accumulation logic
DP2: Periodic mass sampling with calibrated weighing and extrapolation to throughput rate over standardized intervals
DP3: Indirect throughput inference from operational parameters (e.g., motor speed, cycle count, material density assumptions) with lookup tables or empirical models
DP4: Hybrid dual-path measurement (volumetric + mass cross-check) with reconciliation logic to validate reported throughput
DP5: Time-stamped event logging of material batches (count, mass, or volume per batch) with post-processing aggregation to compute throughput rates
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP4: Hybrid dual-path measurement (volumetric + mass cross-check) with reconciliation logic to validate reported throughput

potential_sources: Volumetric and mass measurement subsystems must share a common time reference and synchronization protocol to avoid temporal misalignment during reconciliation.

Reconciliation logic depends on material density assumptions; if density varies significantly across material types or conditions, coupling between measurement method selection and material characterization emerges.
Reporting interface must accommodate both measurement paths and reconciliation status; poor abstraction here can couple reporting format to internal measurement architecture.
Acceptance criteria ('≥ productivity_target') requires clear definition of which measurement path (or reconciled value) is authoritative; ambiguity here introduces coupling to acceptance test design.
coupling_check.notes: DP4 is selected because it decouples the risk of single-point measurement failure and provides independent validation. Volumetric and mass paths can be designed as independent subsystems with a thin reconciliation layer. Coupling is manageable if time synchronization, density assumptions, and reporting semantics are explicitly specified in the DP-to-IM transition. DP1 (continuous volumetric only) risks systematic bias if sensor drift occurs undetected; DP2 (periodic sampling) may miss transient throughput variations; DP3 (indirect inference) is brittle to operational parameter variability; DP5 (batch logging) introduces latency in throughput reporting.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Material density is known or can be reliably estimated. Risk: If density varies unpredictably (e.g., moisture content, compaction), mass-to-volume reconciliation will fail. Mitigation: Specify density measurement or validation protocol as a prerequisite.

Assumption: Both volumetric and mass sensors can be installed and maintained without disrupting operations. Risk: Installation complexity or maintenance burden may exceed worksite capacity. Mitigation: Conduct feasibility study during design phase; consider retrofit vs. new-build constraints.

Assumption: Time synchronization between volumetric and mass measurement subsystems is achievable to within acceptable tolerance (e.g., ±1 second). Risk: Distributed measurement points may lack synchronized clocks, leading to spurious reconciliation failures. Mitigation: Specify clock synchronization mechanism (e.g., NTP, GPS, or local master clock) in DP-to-IM mapping.

Assumption: Acceptance criteria 'productivity_target' is defined as a single scalar value. Risk: If target varies by material type, shift, or operational mode, reporting logic must support conditional thresholds. Mitigation: Clarify whether target is fixed or context-dependent; if context-dependent, specify how context is captured and reported.

Assumption: Worksite safety plan permits installation of measurement infrastructure without introducing new hazards. Risk: Sensors or sampling points may create pinch points, electrical hazards, or obstruct emergency egress. Mitigation: Integrate safety review into DP-to-IM design phase; ensure compliance with site-specific safety policies.

Assumption: Reported throughput is required in real-time or near-real-time. Risk: If reporting latency is not specified, reconciliation logic may introduce unacceptable delay. Mitigation: Define acceptable reporting latency (e.g., 1 minute, 5 minutes) as a constraint on DP4 reconciliation algorithm.

Assumption: Measurement accuracy and repeatability are sufficient to distinguish true throughput variations from sensor noise. Risk: If noise floor is high relative to target precision, reconciliation may oscillate or mask real changes. Mitigation: Specify required measurement uncertainty (e.g., ±5% of target) and validate sensor selection against this requirement.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: (1) Required reporting latency (e.g., 'throughput reported every 5 minutes'); (2) Measurement uncertainty acceptance criterion (e.g., '±5% of productivity_target'); (3) Material type(s) and expected density range(s); (4) Whether productivity_target is fixed or context-dependent (by material, shift, mode); (5) Definition of 'representative operations' (duration, material mix, operational profile). These refinements will reduce ambiguity in DP4 reconciliation logic and acceptance test design. Additionally, confirm that worksite safety plan explicitly permits installation of volumetric and mass measurement infrastructure, and identify any constraints on sensor placement, power supply, or data transmission that may affect IM feasibility.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized measurement validation and throughput reconciliation engine that integrates volumetric and mass-based flow measurement paths, applies cross-check logic, and produces validated throughput telemetry for downstream consumers.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives raw volumetric and mass measurement data from Vehicle sensors; outputs validated/reconciled throughput telemetry

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives measurement configuration and reconciliation strategy parameters (e.g., tolerance thresholds, weighting factors for dual-path validation)

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Emits alerts when measurement discrepancy exceeds safety-critical thresholds or reconciliation fails

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: NGHE Solution is the logical home for measurement validation and data reconciliation logic. Volumetric and mass cross-check algorithms are core to throughput assurance and belong in the centralized solution layer. Minimal diffusion: only telemetry I/O and safety alerts escape the boundary; reconciliation logic remains encapsulated.

Poor fit: Vehicle module typically owns sensor hardware and raw data acquisition, not algorithmic validation. Placing reconciliation logic in Vehicle creates tight coupling between hardware and business logic, violates separation of concerns, and makes algorithm updates vehicle-dependent. High diffusion: validation results must propagate back to NGHE Solution for use.

Very poor fit: Teleoperation is a user interface and remote control layer, not a measurement processing layer. Reconciliation logic has no semantic relationship to teleoperation concerns. Placing DP here creates severe architectural incoherence and forces all measurement validation to diffuse through teleoperation pathways.

Weak fit: While Safety Services may consume reconciliation results and trigger safety responses, it should not own the measurement validation logic itself. Safety Services is a consumer of validated data, not the producer. Placing DP here inverts the dependency and couples safety logic to measurement algorithms.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The dual-path measurement reconciliation logic (volumetric + mass cross-check with validation) is a core data integrity and throughput assurance function that belongs in the centralized NGHE Solution module. This module is the natural home for algorithmic validation, data fusion, and business logic that operates on telemetry streams. Containment is strong because: (1) reconciliation is a pure algorithmic concern independent of hardware or UI; (2) inputs (raw measurements) and outputs (validated throughput) flow naturally through Telemetry interface; (3) configuration and thresholds flow through Tasking; (4) safety escalation flows through SafetyAlert. No major diffusion occurs.
containment_risks: If measurement sources (volumetric and mass sensors) are distributed across multiple Vehicle subsystems, data aggregation latency could impact reconciliation timeliness—mitigate by ensuring Telemetry interface provides synchronized, timestamped dual-path inputs.

If reconciliation thresholds or weighting factors require frequent tuning based on field conditions, Tasking interface must support dynamic parameter updates without requiring NGHE Solution redeployment.

If Safety Services has independent measurement validation logic, duplication and divergence risk arises—establish clear ownership: NGHE Solution owns reconciliation; Safety Services consumes and acts on results.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The FR requirement to 'enable measurement and reporting of material throughput in volumetric or mass units per unit time' is directly verifiable against acceptance criteria 'reported productivity ≥ productivity_target over evaluation period.' The IM boundary (NGHE Solution) owns the reconciliation logic that produces validated throughput telemetry, which feeds downstream productivity reporting. Measurement outputs are observable and quantifiable.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. NGHE Solution is the single authoritative owner of dual-path measurement reconciliation. The Telemetry interface explicitly defines input (raw volumetric + mass data from Vehicle sensors) and output (validated throughput telemetry) contracts. No ambiguity about which module is responsible for FR satisfaction.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP4 (hybrid dual-path measurement with reconciliation logic) directly and sufficiently addresses the FR. The DP specifies both the measurement approach (volumetric + mass cross-check) and the validation mechanism (reconciliation logic), which together enable reliable throughput reporting. The DP is solution-neutral (does not prescribe implementation details) and is logically complete for the FR scope.

diffusion_risks: Data synchronization latency: If volumetric and mass measurement sources are distributed across multiple Vehicle subsystems with independent acquisition timing, reconciliation accuracy depends on synchronized, timestamped dual-path inputs. Mitigate by enforcing Telemetry interface contract to provide time-aligned measurement pairs; establish maximum acceptable skew tolerance in Tasking parameters.

Parameter tuning diffusion: If reconciliation thresholds (tolerance, weighting factors) require frequent field-based tuning, dynamic Tasking updates must be supported without NGHE Solution redeployment. Risk: ad-hoc threshold changes scattered across operational procedures instead of centralized configuration. Mitigate by implementing versioned, auditable parameter management in Tasking interface.

Duplicate validation logic: If Safety Services or Vehicle subsystems independently implement measurement validation (e.g., sensor health checks, range validation), divergence between NGHE Solution reconciliation and distributed validation logic could mask or contradict safety-critical discrepancies. Mitigate by establishing clear ownership: NGHE Solution owns reconciliation; Safety Services consumes validated results and triggers safety responses only.

Alert propagation coupling: SafetyAlert interface creates a hard dependency between NGHE Solution and Safety Services. If safety alert thresholds differ from reconciliation thresholds, or if Safety Services cannot act on alerts in real-time, measurement validation results may not translate to timely safety response. Mitigate by defining alert semantics and response SLAs in SafetyAlert interface contract.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment (0.92 score justified). NGHE Solution is the semantically correct home for DP4 because: (1) Reconciliation logic is a pure algorithmic concern independent of hardware or UI layers; (2) Inputs (raw dual-path measurements) and outputs (validated throughput) flow naturally through Telemetry interface with clear directionality; (3) Configuration and strategy parameters flow through Tasking, enabling algorithm tuning without code changes; (4) Safety escalation flows through SafetyAlert, maintaining separation of concerns. Minimal diffusion: only I/O and alerts escape the boundary; core reconciliation logic remains encapsulated. Alternative placements (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) all exhibit poor fit and high diffusion due to semantic mismatch or inverted dependencies.

recommended_revisions: Formalize Telemetry interface contract to specify: (a) measurement data schema (volumetric units, mass units, timestamps, sensor IDs); (b) synchronization guarantee (maximum acceptable time skew between dual-path inputs); (c) data quality metadata (sensor confidence, validity flags); (d) output schema for validated throughput (units, reconciliation status, confidence score).

Extend Tasking interface to support dynamic parameter updates: (a) reconciliation tolerance thresholds (volumetric-to-mass deviation limits); (b) weighting factors for dual-path validation (e.g., mass-weighted vs. volumetric-weighted averaging); (c) versioning and audit trail for parameter changes; (d) rollback capability for failed parameter updates.

Define SafetyAlert interface semantics: (a) alert severity levels (warning, critical, shutdown); (b) trigger conditions (reconciliation failure, discrepancy exceeds threshold, sensor fault); (c) response SLA (time to Safety Services acknowledgment); (d) alert payload (measurement values, discrepancy magnitude, recommended action).

Establish measurement source aggregation protocol: Ensure Vehicle Telemetry interface provides synchronized, timestamped dual-path inputs (volumetric + mass) from all relevant sensors. If sources are distributed, implement a Vehicle-level aggregation layer that timestamps and buffers measurements for atomic delivery to NGHE Solution.

Document reconciliation algorithm ownership and versioning: Specify that NGHE Solution owns the reconciliation logic and that algorithm updates (tolerance tuning, weighting adjustments) are managed through Tasking parameters, not through Vehicle or Safety Services modifications. Establish change control process for algorithm updates.

Add acceptance criterion mapping: Link the FR acceptance criterion ('reported productivity ≥ productivity_target') to NGHE Solution's validated throughput output. Define how throughput measurements are aggregated into productivity metrics and how measurement uncertainty is accounted for in productivity reporting.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence, and the IM containment score (0.92) is excellent. However, the safety criticality of this FR (high) combined with the identified diffusion risks—particularly data synchronization latency, duplicate validation logic, and alert propagation coupling—requires explicit architectural hardening at the current level before decomposition proceeds. The recommended_revisions identify critical interface contracts and ownership boundaries that must be formalized to prevent safety-critical divergence during implementation. Proceeding to sub-FR decomposition without resolving these interface semantics risks creating lower-level FRs that inherit ambiguous or conflicting safety assumptions. The FR itself is well-scoped and the DP is sound, but the IM realization pathway requires tightening to ensure safety-critical measurement validation cannot be undermined by distributed logic or parameter drift.

revise.why: The current DP4 ('hybrid dual-path measurement with reconciliation logic') is logically complete but insufficiently prescriptive about safety-critical constraints. Revise DP4 to explicitly include: (1) synchronized dual-path input requirement with maximum acceptable time-skew tolerance; (2) reconciliation failure detection and escalation semantics; (3) parameter versioning and audit trail for threshold changes; (4) measurement confidence scoring and uncertainty propagation. This revision elevates DP4 from a functional specification to a safety-aware design parameter that constrains IM realization and prevents diffusion of validation logic into Safety Services or Vehicle subsystems.

assumptions: Vehicle Telemetry interface can provide synchronized, timestamped dual-path measurements (volumetric + mass) with acceptable time-skew (assumed ≤100ms; to be confirmed in Vehicle subsystem design).
NGHE Solution has exclusive ownership of reconciliation logic; Safety Services and Vehicle subsystems do not independently re-validate measurements.
Tasking interface supports dynamic parameter updates without NGHE Solution redeployment; parameter changes are versioned and auditable.
SafetyAlert interface provides real-time alert propagation with defined SLAs; Safety Services can act on alerts within acceptable latency (assumed ≤500ms; to be confirmed in Safety Services design).

Measurement uncertainty and reconciliation confidence scores are propagated downstream to productivity reporting; acceptance criterion 'reported productivity ≥ productivity_target' accounts for measurement uncertainty.
open_risks: Data synchronization latency: If Vehicle subsystems acquire volumetric and mass measurements asynchronously with skew >acceptable tolerance, reconciliation accuracy degrades. Mitigation: Formalize Telemetry interface contract with maximum time-skew requirement; implement Vehicle-level aggregation layer if needed.

Duplicate validation logic diffusion: If Safety Services or Vehicle subsystems independently implement measurement validation (sensor health, range checks), divergence from NGHE Solution reconciliation could mask safety-critical discrepancies. Mitigation: Establish clear ownership boundary; Safety Services consumes validated results only; Vehicle subsystems provide raw measurements only.

Parameter tuning drift: If reconciliation thresholds (tolerance, weighting) are tuned ad-hoc in field operations without centralized versioning, parameter divergence across deployments could cause inconsistent safety responses. Mitigation: Implement versioned, auditable parameter management in Tasking interface; establish change control process.

Alert propagation SLA violation: If SafetyAlert interface does not guarantee timely delivery to Safety Services, or if Safety Services cannot act on alerts within acceptable latency, measurement validation results may not translate to timely safety response. Mitigation: Define alert semantics and response SLAs in SafetyAlert interface contract; validate Safety Services response latency in integration testing.

Measurement confidence scoring ambiguity: If reconciliation confidence scores are not clearly defined or propagated downstream, productivity reporting may not account for measurement uncertainty, leading to false confidence in throughput metrics. Mitigation: Define confidence scoring algorithm in DP; ensure confidence scores are included in validated throughput output; link to productivity acceptance criterion.
evidence_needed: Telemetry interface contract specification: measurement data schema, synchronization guarantee (maximum time-skew), data quality metadata, validated throughput output schema.
Tasking interface extension specification: dynamic parameter update mechanism, versioning and audit trail, rollback capability, parameter change control process.
SafetyAlert interface semantics specification: alert severity levels, trigger conditions, response SLAs, alert payload schema.
Vehicle subsystem design confirmation: dual-path measurement sources, acquisition timing, aggregation layer (if needed), timestamp synchronization mechanism.
Safety Services design confirmation: alert reception and processing latency, response SLA compliance, independence from Vehicle subsystem validation logic.
Reconciliation algorithm specification: tolerance thresholds, weighting factors, confidence scoring algorithm, failure detection and escalation logic.
Acceptance criterion mapping: link between 'reported productivity ≥ productivity_target' and NGHE Solution validated throughput output; measurement uncertainty accounting in productivity metrics.
Integration test plan: data synchronization latency validation, reconciliation accuracy under nominal and fault conditions, alert propagation SLA verification, parameter update rollback testing.
